Title: A SHORT PRIMER ON ABA BIOSYNTHESIS, RELEASE, AND CIRCULATION IN PLANTS

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating ABA release from conjugated ABA pools via beta-glucosidases could improve rapid drought responsiveness and stress tolerance, but likely needs tissue-specific or stress-inducible deployment to avoid constitutive ABA overaccumulation and growth penalties.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Beta-glucosidase deficiency lowers active ABA in leaves and causes stress-sensitive phenotypes, indicating that enzymatic release of ABA from ABA-glucose ester promotes effective ABA accumulation/signaling during dehydration.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; ABA quantification; biochemical beta-glucosidase activity assays in microsomes under water deficit.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Dehydration; water deficit; unstressed vs stressed leaves.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Active in leaves/microsomes/endoplasmic reticulum-associated fractions during dehydration; rapid activity increase under water deficit.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: ABA can circulate as inactive ABA-glucose ester in xylem, vacuole, apoplast, and ER-associated pools; ER transport mechanism of ABA-GE is unknown; stress triggers beta-glucosidase polymerization in microsomes with ~4-fold activity increase.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Constitutive activation may perturb ABA homeostasis and growth; exact isoform-specific effects not resolved in this review.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABA-glucose ester serves as an inactive transport/storage form of ABA. 2. Apoplastic and ER-associated β-glucosidases rapidly release active ABA, especially during dehydration. 3. Arabidopsis mutants deficient in β-glucosidases show lower leaf ABA and stress-sensitive phenotypes, supporting their role in ABA homeostasis.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves; apoplast; cytosol after release from ER/apoplastic/vacuolar conjugate pools
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Drought and osmotic stress signaling; stomatal closure; seed dormancy and germination regulation; stress adaptation.

Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Reporter deployment with ABA-responsive promoters is useful to map ABA distribution and guide tissue-targeted engineering of ABA metabolism/signaling, but these promoters are sensors rather than metabolic genes.
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AtHB6 promoter activity reports ABA-responsive tissues and is negatively regulated by ABI1; used to infer localized ABA pools and redistribution during water stress.
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Promoter-reporter fusion assays (GUS/luciferase); spatial expression mapping; response to exogenous ABA and water deprivation.
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Water deprivation; exogenous ABA; non-stress background.
Common_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Reporter activity in shoot apical meristem, veins, hydathodes, guard cells, root columella cells, and quiescent center; induced in guard cells and leaf vasculature after root water deprivation.
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AtHB6 is discussed as an ABA-responsive homeodomain TF and as a sensor promoter used to infer ABA circulation dynamics rather than a direct ABA metabolic enzyme.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reporter sensitivity may not capture all ABA pools or intermediate distribution.
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. pAtHB6 is a sensitive ABA-responsive promoter used to track ABA distribution in planta. 2. Reporter activity reveals spatially restricted ABA pools under non-stress and stress conditions. 3. AtHB6 is a target of ABI1-mediated negative regulation in ABA signaling.
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves, guard cells, vascular tissues, root tip-associated cells as inferred by reporter activity

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ABA4 is a dehydration-relevant upstream control point in plastid xanthophyll conversion; useful for stress-responsive engineering of ABA production, but uncertain catalytic mechanism and limited effect under non-stress caution against constitutive manipulation alone.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Proposed to mediate or enable conversion of all-trans-violaxanthin to all-trans-neoxanthin in plastids; may bind substrate rather than catalyze directly.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: aba4 mutants show reduced ABA accumulation under water stress, indicating ABA4 promotes de novo ABA synthesis during dehydration.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Positional cloning; mutant phenotyping; stress-induced ABA quantification; chloroplast proteome localization; biochemical inference from pathway position.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms upstream precursor pool for ABA via neoxanthin
Pathway_Genes.Enzyme_Name: Putative neoxanthin synthase / ABA4-associated neoxanthin-forming factor
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not detailed; function emphasized during dehydration.
Pathway_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: Not specified; possible requirement for regulatory subunits inferred
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Upstream-midstream plastid step before NCED cleavage
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Chloroplast envelope proteome (Ferro et al., 2003); no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: In vitro catalytic activity with recombinant E. coli-expressed ABA4 was not demonstrated; active form may require post-translational modification or partner proteins.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Xanthophyll branch leading from violaxanthin/neoxanthin to ABA precursors
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Constitutive modification could disturb carotenoid/xanthophyll balance; actual catalytic role remains uncertain.
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Small conserved chloroplast envelope protein (~17 kDa)
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABA4 was identified by positional cloning as the missing factor for violaxanthin-to-neoxanthin conversion. 2. aba4 mutants accumulate less ABA during dehydration, linking ABA4 to stress-induced de novo ABA synthesis. 3. Direct catalytic activity remains unresolved, suggesting a possible cofactor, scaffold, or modified enzyme role.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves and vascular-associated tissues under dehydration; whole-plant ABA pools
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stress signaling, stomatal regulation, developmental control.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: NCED is the first committed ABA biosynthetic step and a prime target for drought-responsive breeding/editing; tissue-specific or inducible upregulation near vasculature/roots may enhance stress adaptation while limiting chronic growth inhibition.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Cleaves 9-cis-epoxycarotenoids (violaxanthin and/or neoxanthin) to produce xanthoxin, the first cytoplasmic precursor of ABA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: NCED activity promotes ABA biosynthesis by committing carotenoid flux into xanthoxin formation; AtNCED2 and AtNCED3 expression marks root biosynthetic competence.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic identification of NCED family; expression profiling; pathway biochemistry; metabolite-pathway assignment.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Committed-step enzyme that directly forms the immediate ABA precursor xanthoxin
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Water stress/dehydration; lateral root initiation context.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: AtNCED2 and AtNCED3 are expressed in the root pericycle at sites of lateral root initiation; several ABA biosynthetic genes are expressed near leaf vascular bundles.
Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Committed step, upstream of cytosolic ABA conversion
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Expression profiling mentioned; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Both violaxanthin and neoxanthin are considered alternative in vivo substrates; cis-isomerase(s) producing NCED substrate remain unknown.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Oxidative carotenoid cleavage branch of ABA pathway
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced NCED may increase ABA and reduce growth, germination, or transpiration; root/shoot source balance is context dependent.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as the first committed step and key control point
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. NCED cleaves cis-epoxycarotenoids to xanthoxin and constitutes the first committed step in ABA biosynthesis. 2. Arabidopsis AtNCED2 and AtNCED3 are expressed in root pericycle at lateral root initiation sites. 3. NCED expression supports local ABA biosynthesis in leaves and roots during stress/development.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves, vascular tissues, roots, lateral root initiation zones
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Drought response, stomatal closure, seed dormancy, root developmental modulation.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ABA2 is a core cytosolic ABA biosynthetic enzyme; engineering could tune ABA production in roots and branching zones, but constitutive elevation may inhibit growth and germination.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Converts xanthoxin to abscisic aldehyde via short-chain dehydrogenase/reductase activity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ABA2 promotes ABA accumulation by catalyzing a downstream cytosolic conversion step; expression in root branching points supports local ABA biosynthesis in roots.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification; expression analysis; pathway assignment from mutant and enzymatic class evidence.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate precursor (abscisic aldehyde) to ABA
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Root development; likely stress-related ABA synthesis context.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Detectable in branching points of lateral and mature roots.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Expression analysis mentioned; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: This review emphasizes spatial expression rather than direct biochemical details.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increased ABA may suppress growth and lateral root elongation despite aiding stress adaptation.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABA2 encodes a short-chain dehydrogenase/reductase in ABA biosynthesis. 2. It mediates conversion of xanthoxin to abscisic aldehyde. 3. Its expression in lateral and mature root branching points supports local ABA biosynthesis in root tissues.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Roots, especially branching points; broader plant ABA pools

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AAO3 is a terminal ABA biosynthesis enzyme and strong engineering target for modulating active ABA formation in roots and vasculature, but requires coordinated management of molybdenum cofactor and sulfurase activity.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Oxidizes abscisic aldehyde to abscisic acid using molybdenum cofactor-dependent aldehyde oxidase activity.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AAO3 enables the final oxidation step to active ABA and is highly expressed in root tips and vascular bundles, supporting localized ABA production.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification; expression profiling; pathway biochemistry.
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Root developmental zones; vascular tissues; stress-related ABA production context.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly expressed in root tips and vascular bundles.
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Expression analysis mentioned; no accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: AAO3 activity requires sulfurated molybdenum cofactor supplied by ABA3.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Terminal oxidation module of ABA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increasing AAO3 alone may be insufficient if molybdenum cofactor activation is limiting.
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Terminal determinant, but not explicitly established as rate-limiting in this review
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AAO3 catalyzes the final oxidation of abscisic aldehyde to ABA. 2. It is highly expressed in root tips and vascular bundles, indicating local ABA synthesis sites. 3. AAO3 requires sulfurated molybdenum cofactor, linking its activity to ABA3.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Root tips; vascular bundles; whole-plant stress-responsive tissues
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Stress signaling, stomatal regulation, dormancy control.

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ABA3 is essential support machinery for terminal ABA production; can be co-engineered with AAO3 to ensure efficient terminal ABA biosynthesis, though broad cofactor effects may cause pleiotropy.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Sulfurates the molybdenum cofactor required for aldehyde oxidase activity, enabling AAO3-mediated oxidation of abscisic aldehyde to ABA.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ABA3 promotes ABA accumulation indirectly by activating AAO3 through molybdenum cofactor sulfurylation.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Gene identification; biochemical assignment of molybdenum cofactor sulfurase function; genetic pathway analysis.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Required cofactor activation for terminal ABA-forming enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Not specifically resolved in this review; relevant under ABA-producing conditions including stress.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Function is indirect but indispensable for active AAO3.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Cofactor maturation module linked to terminal ABA biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Perturbation may affect other molybdoenzymes beyond ABA pathway.
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Molybdenum cofactor precursor; sulfur donor not specified
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABA3 encodes the molybdenum cofactor sulfurase required for AAO3 activity. 2. It indirectly supports the final conversion of abscisic aldehyde to ABA. 3. ABA3 is therefore an essential enabling component of ABA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Whole-plant ABA pools where AAO3 is active

Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ABA1 is an upstream xanthophyll epoxidase influencing precursor supply for ABA; could be used to alter ABA under stress, but because it intersects broader carotenoid metabolism, deployment should consider pleiotropic effects on photoprotection and pigment composition.
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Epoxidizes zeaxanthin in the xanthophyll pathway to generate epoxycarotenoid ABA precursors (via violaxanthin).
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ABA1 supports ABA precursor formation in plastids by producing epoxyxanthophyll substrates required for downstream NCED cleavage.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic identification of Arabidopsis gene and tobacco ortholog; pathway mapping.
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for ABA synthesis
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: General ABA biosynthesis context; not specifically dissected here.
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Same as applied species / tobacco ortholog also mentioned.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes Arabidopsis ABA1 and tobacco ortholog encode epoxidase.
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Xanthophyll epoxidation branch feeding ABA pathway
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation may alter carotenoid/xanthophyll homeostasis and photoprotection.
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABA1 encodes the epoxidase responsible for forming epoxyxanthophyll ABA precursors. 2. This reaction occurs in plastids upstream of NCED cleavage. 3. ABA1 therefore contributes precursor supply for ABA biosynthesis.
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Stress-responsive tissues including leaves and roots

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: FCA may be useful for modulating ABA-linked flowering and lateral root sensitivity, but because effects on classical ABA responses are limited and flowering pathways are strongly impacted, it is unsuitable as a broad ABA-engineering target without highly specific deployment.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: FCA binds ABA and modulates ABA sensitivity mainly in lateral roots and flowering-related responses rather than broad ABA physiology.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: ABA-binding assays; protein interaction assays with FY; mutant phenotyping; transcriptome analysis; lateral root ABA response assays.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent ABA receptor candidate affecting specific ABA outputs, especially flowering delay and lateral root ABA sensitivity.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Exogenous ABA; lateral root ABA inhibition context.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in lateral root tips; nuclear protein.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Negative feedback loop via FCA-FY control of FCA transcript splicing; ABA disrupts this interaction.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: ABA perception/signaling and developmental response output
Regulation_Genes.Omics_Data: Transcriptome analysis mentioned (Marquardt et al., 2006); no accession reported
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Despite ABA binding, fca lacks most classical ABA phenotypes except lateral roots, leaving receptor status uncertain; possible functional redundancy with FCA-like homologs.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong flowering-time effects; limited general ABA gain; possible redundancy masks outcomes.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: FY-dependent FCA transcript splicing; downstream FLC accumulation indirectly
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Forms complex with FY in nucleus; ABA disrupts FCA-FY interaction
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Nuclear RNA-binding protein with WW domain
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: ABA-dependent disruption of protein-protein interaction affecting RNA processing
Regulation_Genes.Regulator_Type: ABA receptor candidate / RNA-binding post-transcriptional regulator
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Modulates FCA splicing feedback and indirectly promotes FLC mRNA accumulation under ABA, delaying flowering
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FCA binds ABA near its C-terminus. 2. ABA disrupts FCA interaction with FY, altering FCA transcript splicing in a feedback loop and linking ABA to flowering delay. 3. fca mutants mainly show reduced ABA sensitivity in lateral roots, so FCA appears to mediate selective rather than universal ABA responses.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Not a biosynthetic determinant; acts on ABA response outputs in lateral roots and reproductive tissues

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ABAR/CHLH is a strong candidate for engineering whole-plant ABA sensitivity and drought-associated traits, but essential chloroplast functions and embryo lethality of null alleles demand partial, dosage-controlled, or tissue-specific manipulation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ABAR positively mediates ABA sensitivity; knock-down reduces ABA responses and overexpression enhances them.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: ABA-binding studies; knock-down and overexpression phenotyping; expression analysis of ABA-inducible genes and ABI1/ABI2; physiological assays for germination, dormancy, transpiration.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major positive ABA receptor candidate/controller with broad effects across germination, dormancy, transpiration, and embryogenesis.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Exogenous ABA; vegetative and seed developmental contexts.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Expressed in all vegetative tissues; essential during embryogenesis.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Same as applied species; Vicia faba homolog also characterized biochemically.
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: ABI1; ABI2 (expression relationship, not direct binding shown here)
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: ABA perception/signaling; links ABA response with chloroplast-associated signaling context
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Protein has dual functions in chlorophyll synthesis/tetrapyrrole signaling and ABA binding; ABA signaling consequences appear independent of chlorophyll synthesis role.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Null mutation is embryo lethal; interference may perturb plastid signaling and tetrapyrrole metabolism.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: ABA-inducible genes; negative regulators ABI1 and ABI2 (expression level effects)
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: H subunit of Mg-chelatase trimeric complex
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Mg-chelatase H subunit / chloroplast ABA-binding protein
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: ABA binding-associated positive signaling; modulates expression of ABA-responsive genes and suppresses negative regulators
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes ABA-inducible genes and represses ABI1/ABI2 expression
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABAR/CHLH binds ABA and acts as a positive regulator of ABA responses. 2. Knock-down mutants are ABA-insensitive, whereas overexpression enhances ABA sensitivity. 3. ABAR is essential for embryogenesis and has dual roles in plastid signaling and ABA perception.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Not a biosynthetic step; mediates ABA signaling across vegetative tissues and seeds

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: OST1 is a high-value target for improving drought resistance and stomatal immunity by strengthening ABA-induced stomatal closure; however, increased closure may reduce CO2 uptake and growth.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: OST1 positively regulates ABA- and osmotic stress-induced stomatal closure; ost1 mutants have impaired ABA response and drought hypersensitivity.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant analysis; kinase activation assays; guard-cell physiological assays; in vitro phosphorylation of downstream TF motifs; pathogen stomatal response studies.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential positive regulator and central kinase switch for ABA signaling in guard cells and stomatal immunity.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ABA treatment; hyperosmotic stress; mild drought; flg22-triggered stomatal closure; pathogen challenge.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Functional in guard cells; activated by ABA and osmotic stress.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: ABA signal transduction controlling stomatal movement and transcriptional stress responses
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Acts upstream of hydrogen peroxide production in stomatal defense; upstream activating kinases remain unknown; may already be a phosphoprotein before stress.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced closure can limit photosynthesis and growth; broad stress signaling activation may alter development.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: AREB1; AREB2; ABI5; guard-cell ion transport processes
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Interacts with ABI1; not otherwise resolved here
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Phosphorylation activation of downstream transcription factors and guard-cell signaling components
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Activates ABA-responsive bZIP transcription factors and promotes guard-cell ABA outputs
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Likely cytoplasm/nucleus in guard-cell signaling context; exact localization not specified here
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. OST1 is activated by ABA and hyperosmotic stress and is essential for stomatal ABA responses. 2. It phosphorylates ABA-responsive bZIP factors in vitro. 3. OST1 also connects ABA signaling to stomatal innate immunity against bacterial entry.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Guard-cell signaling sites rather than metabolic accumulation sites
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Drought adaptation, stomatal control, pathogen defense
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: ABA; hyperosmotic stress; flg22-linked immune signaling context

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ABI1 is a major brake on ABA signaling; weakening ABI1 can enhance drought tolerance and pathogen resistance, but may cause excessive ABA sensitivity and growth penalties.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ABI1 negatively regulates ABA sensitivity; loss-of-function causes ABA hypersensitivity, while dominant abi1-1 causes ABA insensitivity and altered pathogen/lateral root phenotypes.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Dominant and loss-of-function mutant analysis; double-mutant analysis with hab1; interaction studies with OST1; PA-binding assays; physiological assays of stomatal closure and ABA responses.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Strong negative regulator/gating node of ABA response intensity across multiple outputs.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Exogenous ABA; drought/water loss; pathogen infection; phosphatidic acid signaling context.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Not specified in detail; broadly involved in ABA signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Potential lipid-mediated sequestration by phosphatidic acid; no transcriptional feedback established here.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: ABA signal transduction affecting stomatal closure, gene expression, pathogen response, and development
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Dominant abi1-1 mutation strongly alters ABA responses; PA binding via Arg73 may sequester ABI1 to plasma membrane; HAB1 and ABI1 appear to control parallel pathways.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reducing ABI1 may impair flexible growth regulation and cause over-response to stress; dominant alleles confer severe ABA insensitivity.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: OST1; AtHB6 and other ABA-responsive outputs
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Associates with OST1; possible membrane sequestration complex via PA
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Protein dephosphorylation / negative regulation; possible sequestration by PA
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Suppresses ABA-responsive signaling and AtHB6-linked transcriptional outputs
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytoplasm/plasma membrane-associated when PA-bound; exact distribution not fully resolved here
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABI1 is a core negative regulator of ABA signaling. 2. It interacts with OST1 and is implicated in suppressing ABA-mediated stomatal closure and transcriptional responses. 3. Phosphatidic acid may inhibit ABI1 by membrane sequestration, providing a lipid control layer in ABA signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Not a biosynthetic determinant; regulates ABA responses in guard cells and other tissues

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ABI2 is another major ABA signaling brake and could be edited for stress resilience, but it also intersects ion homeostasis pathways, so deployment requires balancing drought tolerance with ionic stress responses.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ABI2 negatively regulates ABA sensitivity; loss of function causes ABA hypersensitivity, while dominant abi2-1 alters ABA and pathogen responses.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Genetic analysis; physiological assays; interaction studies in related CIPK/SOS pathways.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Major negative regulator of ABA response with overlap but non-identical behavior relative to ABI1.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ABA treatment; pathogen challenge; ion/salt stress context.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: ABA signaling and ion homeostasis-related signal integration
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Unlike ABI1, ABI2 is not reported here to bind OST1; participates in SOS2/SOS3 complex context.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Modification may disrupt salt/ion homeostasis and cause complex stress phenotypes.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Ion-transport-related signaling modules; ABA response outputs
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABI2 is a genetically defined negative regulator of ABA signaling. 2. abi2 mutations alter ABA sensitivity and pathogen responses. 3. ABI2 also interfaces with CBL-CIPK ion homeostasis modules, linking ABA to transporter regulation.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: HAB1 overexpression reduces ABA sensitivity and increases pathogen resistance, suggesting use in disease contexts; however, weakened ABA signaling may compromise drought tolerance and water-use efficiency.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: HAB1 is a negative regulator of ABA signaling; loss-of-function increases ABA sensitivity, whereas 35S::HAB1 reduces ABA sensitivity and pathogen susceptibility outcomes differ.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: T-DNA mutant analysis; double-mutant analysis with abi1; overexpression phenotyping; pathogen assays.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Parallel negative controller of ABA signaling intensity.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: ABA signaling strength across stress and defense outputs
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: hab1-1 and abi1 loss-of-function double mutants are more ABA sensitive than singles, implying parallel pathway control rather than simple redundancy.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Overexpression may undermine drought responses while improving some pathogen resistance phenotypes.
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. HAB1 is a negative regulator of ABA signaling. 2. Loss of HAB1 increases ABA sensitivity, and HAB1 acts in parallel with ABI1. 3. Constitutive HAB1 overexpression lowers ABA sensitivity and affects pathogen resistance.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: FsPP2C may be explored as a positive ABA-sensitizing transgene for drought adaptation, but dwarfism and delayed flowering indicate strong pleiotropic costs.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Overexpression enhances ABA sensitivity in seeds and vegetative tissues and causes dwarfism and delayed flowering.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Heterologous overexpression under 35S promoter; phenotyping; GA3 rescue assays.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Positive regulator candidate for ABA sensitivity, notable because it contrasts with many Arabidopsis PP2Cs.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Driven constitutively in transgenic Arabidopsis by 35S promoter.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Source gene isolated from beech; endogenous pattern not detailed here.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Positive PP2C role remains heterologous and no endogenous Arabidopsis positive PP2C had been identified at the time of review.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Dwarf phenotype and delayed flowering; likely broad developmental penalty.
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Positive modulation of ABA sensitivity (mechanism unresolved)
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. FsPP2C overexpression in Arabidopsis enhances ABA sensitivity. 2. It causes dwarfism and delayed flowering, reversible by GA3. 3. FsPP2C contrasts with canonical Arabidopsis PP2Cs, suggesting mechanistic diversity among ABA-related PP2Cs.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ABI3 is a strong node for ABA-driven seed maturation and miR159 induction, and also affects lateral root development; applications must consider profound seed and developmental effects.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ABI3 promotes ABA-responsive developmental and transcriptional programs and is required for ABA-induced miR159 accumulation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Mutant phenotyping; transcriptional analyses; seed maturation and lateral root studies; miRNA expression studies.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential B3-domain activator for major ABA developmental outputs and upstream controller of ABA-induced miRNA homeostasis.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ABA treatment; seed maturation; lateral root auxin/ABA contexts.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Highly characterized in seeds; also expressed in vegetative tissues including roots.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Same as applied species; orthologous relation to maize VP1 noted.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feeds forward to miR159 accumulation, which then dampens ABA signaling by degrading positive regulators.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: ABA-responsive developmental programming and post-transcriptional homeostasis
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Null or strong mutants affect seed maturation; embryonic defects of abar null resemble abi3, highlighting pathway convergence.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong developmental and seed phenotypes; altered auxin interaction in roots.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: miR159 accumulation; seed maturation gene programs
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes ABA-responsive genes and enables ABA-induced miR159 accumulation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABI3 is a core B3 transcriptional activator in ABA developmental signaling. 2. ABA-induced accumulation of miR159 depends on ABI3. 3. ABI3 also participates in lateral root development and broader vegetative ABA responses.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: miR159 module can be leveraged to tune ABA sensitivity homeostatically during seedling stress responses; overactivation may compromise germination-related vigor through repression of positive regulators.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: miR159 accumulation, induced by ABA, desensitizes ABA signaling by cleaving transcripts of positive regulators MYB101 and MYB33.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression analysis under drought/hormones; dependence on ABI3; target cleavage evidence.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Homeostatic negative feedback regulator that tempers ABA response intensity.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Feedback/homeostatic repression of ABA signaling via degradation of positive regulators.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Review notes miR159 as one of the clearest mechanistic links between RNA metabolism and ABA signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Excess miR159 may reduce desirable ABA sensitivity during germination or stress establishment.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Acts through RNA silencing machinery; specific complex not detailed here
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. miR159 is induced by drought and ABA. 2. ABI3 is required for ABA-induced miR159 accumulation. 3. miR159 cleaves MYB101 and MYB33 transcripts, likely providing homeostatic desensitization of ABA signaling.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AAPK is a guard-cell ABA signaling kinase with potential to enhance stomatal responsiveness and drought tolerance in legumes, but stronger closure may limit gas exchange.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AAPK positively mediates ABA signaling in guard cells and can phosphorylate the RNA-binding protein AKIP.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Biochemical purification; kinase activation by ABA; dominant-negative P-loop mutant assay in transformed guard cells; in vitro phosphorylation assays.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Pivotal early positive kinase in guard-cell ABA signal transduction in Vicia faba.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ABA treatment; guard-cell drought signaling context.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: ABA signal transduction and post-transcriptional drought response in guard cells
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Vicia AAPK- AKIP relationship appears not fully conserved in Arabidopsis OST1-UBA2 module.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Constitutive activation may reduce stomatal opening and photosynthetic efficiency.
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirectly promotes drought-associated mRNA regulation via AKIP and guard-cell ABA responses
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AAPK is activated by ABA in guard cells. 2. Kinase-dead dominant-negative forms block ABA responses, supporting an essential signaling role. 3. AAPK phosphorylates AKIP, linking ABA signaling to RNA-associated regulation.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: AKIP-like RNA-binding modules could be used to enhance post-transcriptional drought responses, but direct downstream transcript specificity remains incompletely defined.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AKIP relays ABA signaling downstream of AAPK and associates with drought-related mRNA, with ABA-induced relocalization to nuclear speckles.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Protein interaction and phosphorylation assays; subcellular relocalization studies; RT-PCR-based mRNA association detection.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent post-transcriptional effector linking ABA-activated kinase signaling to RNA regulation.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Constitutively nuclear localized; reorganizes to nuclear speckles after ABA activation.
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Known bound transcript includes a dehydrin mRNA; broader RNA target spectrum remains unresolved.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad manipulation of RNA-binding regulators may cause pleiotropic transcriptome effects.
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Phosphorylation-dependent RNA-associated regulation and subnuclear reorganization
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Likely stabilizes or modulates processing/localization of drought-associated transcripts
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Nucleus; nuclear speckles after ABA activation
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. AKIP is an ABA-responsive RNA-binding protein phosphorylated by AAPK. 2. It binds at least one dehydrin mRNA. 3. ABA induces AKIP reorganization into nuclear speckles, linking signaling to post-transcriptional control.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: ABF/AREB bZIP factors are central transcriptional activators for ABA-responsive genes and valuable for engineering stress-inducible programs, though constitutive activation can impair growth.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: AREB1, AREB2, ABI5, ABF2, ABF3, and ABF4 are phosphorylated/activated by ABA-related kinases and drive ABA-responsive transcription.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: In vitro phosphorylation assays by SnRK2s and CPK32; interaction assays; transient expression transactivation assays.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Core transcriptional executors of ABA signaling that convert kinase activity into ABA-responsive gene expression.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Exogenous ABA; osmotic stress; calcium signaling context.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: ABA-responsive transcription factors; tissue details not fully specified in this review.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Same as applied species; barley HVABI5 homolog also studied.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Not established here; likely feedforward amplification of ABA-responsive gene expression.
Regulation_Genes.Gene_Name: AREB1; AREB2; ABI5; ABF2; ABF3; ABF4
Regulation_Genes.Interacting_Proteins: OST1; SnRK2.2; SnRK2.3; SnRK2.8; AtCPK32; AtCDPK10; AtCDPK30; 14-3-3 proteins (HVABI5 context)
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Transcriptional activation of ABA-responsive stress programs
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Bind ABRE promoter motifs; C-domain phosphorylation motifs contain Arg at −3; combinatorial regulation by SnRK2 and CPK suggested.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Constitutive activation may reduce growth, alter germination, and exaggerate stress programs.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Can bind 14-3-3 proteins (HVABI5 evidence); form transcriptional complexes via bZIP dimerization
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABA-responsive bZIP transcription factors are direct kinase targets in ABA signaling. 2. SnRK2s and CPK32 phosphorylate conserved C-domain motifs to enhance transactivation. 3. These TFs bind ABRE elements to induce ABA/stress-responsive gene expression.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CBL9/CIPK1/CIPK23 nodes can tune ABA and nutrient/ion responses simultaneously, potentially valuable for drought-low K+ environments, but tradeoffs with ion homeostasis are likely.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of CBL9 or CIPK1 leads to ABA hypersensitivity; CBL1/CBL9 with CIPK23 regulate AKT1 and integrate calcium, potassium nutrition, and ABA signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Suppressor/mutant analysis; protein interaction studies; transporter phosphorylation/regulation assays.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Condition-dependent modulators integrating ABA with calcium and ion transport signals.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Ion transport regulation and ABA sensitivity integration
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: CBL1 may have ABA-independent functions; CBL-CIPK combinations likely operate as network modules rather than simple linear pathways.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Manipulation may perturb K+ uptake and salt tolerance while altering ABA sensitivity.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: AKT1 potassium transporter; plasma membrane ion transport modules
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Calcineurin B-like calcium sensors and CIPK/SnRK3 kinases
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Calcium-dependent complex formation and phosphorylation control
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Primarily regulates transporter activity rather than direct gene transcription
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CBL9 suppression causes hypersensitivity to low K+ and ABA. 2. CBL1/CBL9 pair with CIPK23 to regulate AKT1 and with CIPK1 in ABA-related signaling. 3. These modules integrate calcium, nutrient transport, and ABA response pathways.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: CPK3 and CPK6 are attractive targets for strengthening ABA-induced stomatal closure and water conservation, but tighter stomata may reduce carbon assimilation.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: CPK3 and CPK6 positively transduce calcium signals required for full ABA activation of S-type anion currents in guard cells.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Insertion mutant analysis; guard-cell electrophysiology; ABA and Ca2+ response assays.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Key Ca2+-decoding kinases enabling ABA-induced guard-cell ion channel activation.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ABA; guard-cell Ca2+ transients; low extracellular Ca2+ conditions.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: ABA appears to prime CDPK-mediated Ca2+ sensing; formal loop not established.
Regulation_Genes.Gene_Accession_Number: CPK3; CPK6 specific accessions not provided
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Guard-cell ion channel activation and stomatal closure
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: May also contribute to regulation of Ca2+-permeable I(Ca) channels; direct phosphorylation of bZIPs not established here.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Increased stomatal closure may decrease photosynthesis and growth.
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: S-type anion channel activation machinery; possibly Ca2+-permeable channels
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Calcium-dependent protein kinases with EF-hand Ca2+-binding domains
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Primarily affects ion channels rather than direct transcriptional targets
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. CPK3 and CPK6 are required for full ABA activation of guard-cell anion currents. 2. Loss of either kinase impairs ABA-induced stomatal signaling. 3. ABA likely primes these CDPKs to decode Ca2+ elevations during stomatal closure.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: PKS5/SCaBP1-mediated inhibition of plasma membrane H+-ATPases offers a route to reinforce ABA-driven stomatal closure; however, altered proton pumping can broadly affect growth and nutrient uptake.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: PKS5 negatively regulates AHA2 proton pump activity via phosphorylation, opposing stomatal opening-associated H+ pumping.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Protein interaction studies; phosphorylation mapping; yeast functional complementation/growth assays with co-transformation.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Important membrane-level regulator connecting calcium signaling to ABA-associated stomatal closure mechanics.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Membrane proton pumping and stomatal movement regulation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Phosphorylation of AHA2 Ser931 interferes with 14-3-3 binding to the activating phosphothreonine near the C-terminus.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad suppression of H+-ATPases may impair nutrient uptake, growth, and opening responses to light.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: PKS5 interacts functionally with calcium-binding protein SCaBP1
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Primarily post-translational regulation of AHA2 rather than direct gene control
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. PKS5 phosphorylates AHA2 at Ser931. 2. This phosphorylation blocks 14-3-3-mediated activation of the proton pump. 3. PKS5 with SCaBP1 therefore provides a mechanism to suppress H+-ATPase activity consistent with ABA-induced stomatal closure.

Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Specific 14-3-3 isoforms and HvABI5 are promising targets for ABA-responsive seed/root trait engineering, but their broad scaffolding roles imply pleiotropic effects.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: ABA prolongs Hv14-3-3D/E abundance, and 14-3-3 proteins support ABA reporter induction partly via interaction with HvABI5.
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Expression and protein level analysis under ABA; RNAi silencing of 14-3-3 isoforms; yeast two-hybrid screening; transient transactivation assays.
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Platform/scaffold regulators that stabilize or modulate ABA signaling proteins, including ABA-responsive bZIP factors.
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: ABA treatment in embryonic barley roots.
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Subset of 14-3-3 isoforms prolonged in embryonic roots upon ABA treatment.
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: ABA induces 14-3-3 expression/protein accumulation, and 14-3-3s in turn support ABA signaling outputs, suggesting feedforward reinforcement.
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: ABA signal amplification/scaffolding and transcriptional output
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: HvABI5 contains two putative 14-3-3-binding motifs important for transactivation.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Because 14-3-3 proteins have many client proteins, manipulation may cause broad signaling changes.
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: 14-3-3 acidic dimeric scaffold proteins; HvABI5 is a bZIP TF
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Protein scaffolding/stabilization and support of transactivation
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Supports ABA-responsive transcription mediated by HvABI5
Regulation_Genes.Subcellular_Localization: Cytoplasm/nucleus depending partner; exact site not specified here
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. ABA increases or prolongs specific 14-3-3 isoforms in barley roots. 2. Silencing any of five 14-3-3 genes impairs ABA reporter induction. 3. HvABI5 interacts with 14-3-3 proteins through motifs required for full transactivation.
